text: There is something quietly beautiful about the way condensation forms on a cold glass on a warm day. Not the glass itself, or even the drink inside, but that thin film of water that gathers from nowhere, from the air itself, drawn out of invisibility by the simple fact of temperature difference. The droplets start small and separate, each one a perfect little lens, and then they find each other and merge, and the merged drops grow heavy and run down the side in crooked, unpredictable paths. It is physics made visible, the atmosphere briefly revealing itself on the surface of something you were about to drink without thinking. You did not put that water there. It came on its own, quietly, out of the surrounding air, as if the glass pulled something hidden into plain sight. There is an honesty to it that I find hard to name but easy to recognize.
